stories.headline: Quantum mechanics once baffled scientists. Now it's changing the world
stories.summary: Quantum mechanics has journeyed from a strange and controversial idea to the foundation of some of humanity’s most advanced technologies. Now researchers are pushing its boundaries even further, with potential breakthroughs in energy, medicine, computing, and our understanding of the universe.

stories.headline: Majority of UK smokers wrongly believe vaping is as harmful as cigarettes, experts find
stories.summary: Analysis shows public understanding about vaping, which is far less harmful than smoking, has plummeted in past decadeMore than half of adult smokers in the UK wrongly believe that vaping is as harmful or more harmful than cigarettes, making them less likely to switch to vapes and quit the deadly habit, research has…
stories.verification: Source pre-vetted (Tier 2 feed: The Guardian — Health)

stories.headline: Scientists discover the deep sleep circuit that builds muscle, burns fat, and boosts the brain
stories.summary: Researchers have identified the brain circuitry that links deep sleep with the release of growth hormone, revealing how the two regulate each other. The newly discovered feedback loop helps explain why poor sleep can interfere with growth, muscle repair, fat metabolism, and brain function. Understanding this system…
stories.verification: Source pre-vetted (Tier 2 feed: ScienceDaily — Mind & Brain)

stories.headline: Light switch wakes sleeping cancer cells and makes them vulnerable again
stories.summary: Some cancer cells evade treatment by entering a dormant state triggered by stress hormones. ETH Zurich scientists have created a light-controlled molecular switch that selectively destroys the receptors responsible for this survival mode. In laboratory lung cancer cells, the approach woke sleeping tumor cells and…

stories.headline: The secret to healthy aging may be hidden in your blood
stories.summary: Some people live past 100 with remarkable health, and researchers may have uncovered one reason why. A new study found that centenarians have a unique chemical "fingerprint" in their blood that sets them apart from normal aging, including unusual patterns of bile acids and steroids linked to longer survival.

stories.headline: A hidden weakness in deadly cancers could lead to powerful new treatments
stories.summary: A UCLA study has identified a hidden Achilles' heel in aggressive small cell cancers that have resisted new treatments for decades. Scientists found that tumors lacking the RB gene become critically dependent on the protein E2F3 for survival. Blocking E2F3 shut down tumor growth in laboratory models, and existing…

stories.headline: Drinking to cope with stress may permanently rewire your brain
stories.summary: Using alcohol to cope with stress when young may permanently alter the brain, making it harder to adapt to challenges and increasing the risk of returning to drinking later in life. Researchers also found signs of brain damage associated with early dementia, suggesting the effects can linger long after alcohol use has…
stories.verification: Source pre-vetted (Tier 2 feed: ScienceDaily — Mind & Brain)

stories.headline: Scientists discover why some brains resist Alzheimer's
stories.summary: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.
stories.verification: Source pre-vetted (Tier 2 feed: ScienceDaily — Mind & Brain)

stories.headline: Stop Calling It Cancer; The Alcohol Paradox; Rural Patients Lose Ground in Screening
stories.summary: (MedPage Today) -- Relabeling the lowest-risk prostate cancers as a non-cancerous condition might reduce overtreatment and prevent as many as 2,400 prostate cancer deaths annually. (UCLA Health) Social media influencer and cookbook author Nara...

stories.headline: Exercise May Not Be a No-No After Aortic Dissection
stories.summary: (MedPage Today) -- It was feasible for survivors of thoracic aortic dissection (TAD) to engage in a structured exercise program, according to a pilot study. Among 93 adults post-Type A or B TAD, there were no deaths, aortic operations, or recurrent...

stories.headline: Why Gen Z bros are ditching their MAGA hats
stories.summary: The numbers are clear: President Donald Trump’s approval rating is cratering across the board, and he’s losing the most ground with groups that were key demographics to his win in 2024. In that election, men under 30 broke for Trump over Vice President Kamala Harris by about a single point — not a lot, but […]

stories.headline: The biological dogma that women don’t make new eggs after birth may be wrong
stories.summary: Female mammals have long thought to be born with all the eggs they would ever have, but new research is challenging that consensus
stories.verification: Source pre-vetted (Tier 2 feed: Scientific American — Health)

stories.headline: Old muscle stem cells can act young again but there’s a catch
stories.summary: Scientists at UCLA discovered a surprising reason aging muscles heal more slowly. In older muscle stem cells, a protein called NDRG1 builds up and acts like a brake, slowing the cells’ ability to jump into repair mode after injury. But there’s a twist: that same protein helps the cells survive the stresses of aging,…

stories.headline: Scientists stunned as bumble bees solve a classic intelligence test
stories.summary: Bumble bees astonished researchers by inventing a new way to reach a hidden reward, despite never being taught the trick. The discovery adds to growing evidence that these tiny insects are far smarter and more adaptable than once believed.
stories.verification: Source pre-vetted (Tier 2 feed: ScienceDaily — Mind & Brain)

stories.headline: Scientists discover a protein switch that burns fat and blocks new fat cells
stories.summary: A protein called “Mitch” may hold the key to a new generation of obesity treatments. Researchers found that disabling it in human cells boosts fat burning, increases energy use, and makes it harder for new fat cells to develop. The findings help explain why mice lacking Mitch were leaner, more athletic, and resistant…
